title: Secure housing or make a backup plan
summary: Confirm where you will sleep for your first weeks and avoid risky last-minute housing decisions.
appliesToLabel: Everyone, especially students without confirmed accommodation
whatThisIs: A practical checkpoint for finding housing, checking contract basics, and preparing a fallback if permanent housing is not ready.
whyItMatters: Housing shortages can affect registration, budget, wellbeing, and the ability to start your studies calmly.
nextSteps: Ask your institution which housing options or warning lists they provide.
Verify the landlord, address, contract terms, deposit, and payment method before transferring money.
Book temporary accommodation only as a short bridge, not as a full housing strategy.

rules.label: No confirmed housing
rules.reason: Without housing, several arrival and registration steps become harder.

title: Check visa and residence permit steps
summary: Understand whether you need an MVV, residence permit, biometrics appointment, or permit pickup.
whatThisIs: A check of your immigration route and the university or IND steps attached to it.
whyItMatters: Missing immigration deadlines can delay travel, enrolment, or legal residence.
nextSteps: Follow the immigration instructions from your university or recognised sponsor.
Check whether an MVV is needed before travel.
Save IND messages, payment proof, and permit collection instructions.

rules.label: Non-EU/EEA/Swiss student
rules.reason: Non-EU/EEA/Swiss students commonly have additional immigration steps.

title: Prepare important documents
summary: Collect, scan, print, and legalise documents you may need for immigration, university, housing, and registration.
whatThisIs: A document folder for identity, admission, housing, insurance, finances, and civil-status records.
whyItMatters: Some documents are difficult to replace or legalise after leaving your home country.
nextSteps: Check university, IND, and municipality document lists.
Arrange legalisation, apostille, or translation where required.
Store scans securely and bring printed copies for appointments.

title: Plan your arrival day
summary: Prepare travel, first-night access, key pickup, temporary money, and transport from the station or airport.
whatThisIs: A first-day logistics plan that covers where you go, who you contact, and how you pay if something is delayed.
whyItMatters: The first day is easier when you do not need to solve luggage, keys, groceries, and transport at the same time.
nextSteps: Save your address, key pickup instructions, and emergency contacts offline.
Plan public transport from your arrival point and a backup option.
Keep payment cards, some emergency cash, and essential documents in hand luggage.

title: Register with the municipality
summary: Book and attend your BRP registration appointment if you will live in the Netherlands long enough to register as a resident.
whatThisIs: Municipality registration records your address in the Personal Records Database and starts the BSN process.
whyItMatters: Registration is tied to your BSN, government letters, healthcare, and many practical services.
nextSteps: Book a municipality appointment as early as possible.
Bring accepted identity, address, and civil-status documents.
Ask the municipality what to do if your housing situation changes.

title: Get and store your BSN
summary: Receive your citizen service number and keep it private but accessible for official processes.
appliesToLabel: Students who register in the Netherlands
whatThisIs: The BSN is your personal number for dealings with Dutch public services and some regulated organisations.
whyItMatters: You may need your BSN for healthcare, work, taxes, banking follow-up, and DigiD.
nextSteps: Check how and when your municipality provides the BSN.
Store the number securely and avoid sharing it casually.
Use it to continue dependent steps such as DigiD or bank account completion.

title: Arrange banking and payment access
summary: Make sure you can pay rent, groceries, transport, subscriptions, and receive refunds or wages.
appliesToLabel: Everyone, especially students staying for a full degree
whatThisIs: A setup task for IBAN, debit card, mobile payments, and backup payment methods.
whyItMatters: Some Dutch payments and subscriptions are easier with an IBAN and local payment method.
nextSteps: Compare banks or payment providers and check document requirements.
Keep a backup card while your Dutch account or card is pending.
Update rent, university, employer, and refund details after your account is ready.

rules.label: No Dutch bank account
rules.reason: Local payment access can reduce friction with rent, refunds, and transport.

title: Set up reliable phone access
summary: Make sure you can be reached for housing, appointments, deliveries, banking, and two-factor authentication.
whatThisIs: A practical task for choosing roaming, prepaid SIM, eSIM, or subscription options.
whyItMatters: Many services assume you can receive SMS, app notifications, or callback calls.
nextSteps: Check whether your current plan has fair and affordable EU roaming.
Compare prepaid, eSIM, and subscription options before signing a contract.
Update important accounts with a number you can reliably access.

rules.label: No reliable Dutch phone number
rules.reason: Reliable phone access helps with appointments and account verification.

title: Confirm the right health insurance
summary: Check whether you need home-country, private student, EHIC, or Dutch basic health insurance.
appliesToLabel: Everyone; rules differ for study, work, and internships
whatThisIs: An insurance decision based on what you do in the Netherlands, not only where you are from.
whyItMatters: Wrong or missing coverage can create large costs and official follow-up letters.
nextSteps: Read student-specific insurance guidance before buying coverage.
Check again before starting a job or internship.
Save insurance policy documents and emergency numbers.

title: Register with a GP
summary: Find a general practitioner near your address for non-urgent care, prescriptions, and referrals.
appliesToLabel: Students with a stable address in the Netherlands
whatThisIs: A healthcare access task for choosing and registering with a local GP practice.
whyItMatters: The GP is usually your first contact for non-urgent medical care and specialist referrals.
nextSteps: Search for GP practices near your postcode.
Ask whether they accept new patients and what documents they need.
Save out-of-hours and emergency instructions after registration.

title: Apply for DigiD
summary: Set up DigiD after you have a BSN and registered address so you can access Dutch online services.
whatThisIs: A digital identity setup task for government, healthcare, and other public-service logins.
whyItMatters: Without DigiD, many official online tasks take longer or require alternative access routes.
nextSteps: Apply after your municipality registration and BSN are ready.
Watch for the activation letter at your registered address.
Activate DigiD before the activation code expires.

rules.label: BSN received
rules.reason: DigiD becomes actionable once the BSN and registered address are available.

title: Set up transport and daily-life basics
summary: Plan how you will move around by public transport, bike, and walking during the first weeks.
whatThisIs: A mobility setup task for route planning, public transport payment, bike basics, and daily errands.
whyItMatters: Reliable transport affects registration appointments, classes, groceries, healthcare, and social life.
nextSteps: Install a public transport planner and test routes to campus and the municipality.
Check whether OVpay, tickets, or an OV-chipkaart fits your situation.
If cycling, budget for a lock, lights, rain gear, and repair costs.
